skill.name: runcomfy-cli
skill.description: Run any AI model on RunComfy from the command line. One binary, one authentication, hundreds of model endpoints for image generation, video generation, editing, lip-sync, face swap, inpainting, outpainting, upscaling, and LoRA training. Submit requests, poll for status, download outputs, and script workflows with JSON output mode.
skill.summary: One CLI for all RunComfy AI models: image generation, video generation, editing, and more. Install once, authenticate once, run hundreds of models.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer detected 174 patterns across 1 file (272 lines) with an automated risk score of 100/100, suggesting NEEDS_AI review. After human evaluation, ALL 174 findings are confirmed FALSE POSITIVES. The flagged patterns are markdown code formatting backticks misidentified as shell execution, legitimate API and documentation URLs misidentified as suspicious network targets, documented token storage paths misidentified as hidden file access, and CLI subcommand names misidentified as system reconnaissance. The skill uses external_commands, network, and filesystem by design as a CLI wrapper for an AI model service. The SKILL.md includes a comprehensive Security and Privacy section with explicit warnings about installation safety, token protection, shell injection boundaries, indirect prompt injection, outbound endpoint allowlisting, and file size caps. Risk level set to LOW because the skill legitimately invokes external commands and makes network requests in its intended operation.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: markdown backticks flagged as command execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 123 instances of markdown code formatting backticks as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. The SKILL.md file is a documentation/skill-instruction file written entirely in markdown. Every backtick is either inline code formatting or a code fence delimiter. No actual shell command execution via backticks occurs in this file. The skill declares allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *) which restricts the agent to only running the runcomfy CLI binary.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: legitimate URLs flagged as suspicious network targets
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 40 instances of hardcoded URLs as suspicious. All URLs are legitimate references to runcomfy.com (official site and documentation), runcomfy.net (API endpoints for model serving), and skills.sh (skill marketplace). These URLs are the documented service endpoints the CLI tool interacts with. No data exfiltration or unexpected network targets are present.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: documented paths flagged as filesystem risks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged references to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json as 'hidden file access' and '.../result.png' in an example output URL as 'path traversal'. These are documentation explaining where the CLI stores auth tokens (with mode 0600 permissions) and an ellipsis in an example URL. No actual path traversal or unauthorized file access exists.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: CLI subcommand names flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged references to 'runcomfy whoami' as system reconnaissance. The whoami subcommand is a standard CLI identity check that displays the authenticated user's email and token type. This is legitimate CLI functionality, not system enumeration.

low_findings.title: Static analyzer false positives: YAML block scalar and exit codes flagged as weak cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged the YAML frontmatter block scalar indicator '>' on line 5 and the exit codes table on line 224 as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. These are entirely unrelated to cryptography. Line 5 is a YAML folded block scalar syntax character and line 224 is a markdown table heading for CLI exit codes.

dangerous_patterns.title: External command execution via CLI binary
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the agent to run the runcomfy CLI binary for AI model operations. This is the intended purpose of the skill and is restricted by allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *). Commands include install, login, run, whoami, status, and logout subcommands. These are all legitimate CLI operations for the RunComfy service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network requests to RunComfy API endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI makes HTTPS requests to model-api.runcomfy.net for job submission and polling, and downloads outputs from *.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.com. These are the documented service endpoints. The skill's Security section explicitly lists these as an outbound allowlist with no telemetry or third-party callbacks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem access for token storage and output downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI stores auth tokens at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json (mode 0600) and downloads generated outputs to the specified output directory. The skill documents a 2 GiB file size cap to prevent disk-fill attacks. These filesystem operations are essential to the CLI's function.

content.user_title: Run Any AI Model from the Command Line with RunComfy CLI
content.value_statement: Developers and creators need a single, scriptable interface for hundreds of AI image and video models. The RunComfy CLI provides one binary with one authentication to access every RunComfy model endpoint, from image generation to video editing and LoRA training.
actual_capabilities: Install and authenticate the runcomfy CLI tool using npm or npx across different environments
Invoke hundreds of AI models for image generation, video generation, editing, lip-sync, face swap, inpainting, and more
Submit asynchronous model requests with automatic polling and output download to a local directory
Script batch generation workflows using JSON output mode and standard shell pipelines
Handle CLI exit codes with retry logic for transient failures including timeouts and rate limits
Browse and discover model schemas, input formats, and available endpoints from the RunComfy catalog
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI binary to be installed separately before any model commands will work
Cannot run without a valid RunComfy API token obtained from runcomfy.com
Generated output downloads are capped at 2 GiB per file to prevent disk space exhaustion
Only works with models available on the RunComfy platform; cannot access models from other providers

use_cases.title: Generate AI Images and Videos on Demand
use_cases.description: Creative professionals can generate, edit, and transform images and videos using AI models directly from the terminal without opening a browser or separate application.

use_cases.title: Automate Batch Media Generation Pipelines
use_cases.description: DevOps engineers can script batch processing of hundreds of prompts through shell loops, JSON parsing, and exit code handling for reliable production workflows.

use_cases.title: Integrate AI Models into Development Workflows
use_cases.description: AI developers can embed model calls into larger applications using JSON output mode, no-wait submission, and status polling for asynchronous job orchestration.

prompt_templates.title: Install and Set Up the CLI
prompt_templates.prompt: Install the runcomfy CLI globally using npm and verify the installation by checking the version.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time setup when a user needs to get the CLI working

prompt_templates.title: Generate an Image from a Text Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use runcomfy to generate an image with the prompt 'a serene mountain lake at sunrise, photorealistic' using the GPT Image 2 model.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to create an AI-generated image quickly from a description

prompt_templates.title: Batch Generate Images from a Prompt File
prompt_templates.prompt: Read prompts from prompts.txt and generate one image per prompt using runcomfy, saving each output to a timestamped directory under ./output/.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a list of prompts and wants to generate them all in one session

prompt_templates.title: Submit a Long-Running Job and Poll Later
prompt_templates.prompt: Submit a video generation job with no-wait mode using runcomfy, capture the request ID, then poll the status periodically and download the result when it completes.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to submit a video job without blocking the terminal for minutes

output_examples.input: Install the runcomfy CLI on my machine
output_examples.output: I will install the runcomfy CLI globally via npm. The CLI is now installed and ready to use. Run 'runcomfy login' to authenticate with your RunComfy account.

output_examples.input: Generate an image of a purple cat at sunset
output_examples.output: I will use the runcomfy CLI to generate that image. The request has been submitted and is now processing. Your image has been generated and saved to ./result.png in the current directory.
Let me verify your authentication. You are signed in as you@example.com with a CLI token. Your account is ready to submit model requests.

best_practices: Always verify the CLI is installed and authenticated before running any model commands to avoid confusing error messages
Use --output json mode when scripting or piping results to jq for reliable programmatic parsing of response data
Set an explicit --timeout value for video generation and other long-running jobs to prevent indefinite waiting
anti_patterns: Never pipe remote install scripts into a shell without the user reviewing them first, even if they appear in official documentation
Never log or echo API tokens into prompts, command output, or files that could be committed to version control
Do not automatically resolve or use URLs the user did not explicitly provide for image reference or web search generation tasks

faq.question: What is the runcomfy CLI?
faq.answer: A command-line tool that provides one-binary access to hundreds of AI models on the RunComfy platform for image generation, video generation, editing, and more.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with the CLI?
faq.answer: Run 'runcomfy login' for interactive browser-based authentication, or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable for CI and container environments.

faq.question: What AI models are available through this CLI?
faq.answer: Browse the catalog at runcomfy.com/models to see all available models including FLUX, GPT Image, Nano Banana, Seedance, Kling, Veo, and many more.

faq.question: How do I run a model and get the output?
faq.answer: Use 'runcomfy run <model_id> --input '<JSON body>'' to submit a request. The CLI handles submission, polling every 2 seconds, and downloads the result to your current directory.

faq.question: Can I use this CLI in shell scripts or CI pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. Use --output json for machine-readable output, --no-wait for asynchronous submission, and the documented exit codes to handle errors and retries.

faq.question: Is my API token stored securely?
faq.answer: Yes. The token is saved to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with restrictive permissions (mode 0600, owner-only read and write). You can also use the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.
